relations.label: Part III: OF A CHRISTIAN COMMON-WEALTH
Part IV: OF THE KINGDOME OF DARKNESSE

memes.label: Abraham
memes.description: Abraham is central to Hobbes' argument in Part III — his covenant with God is the model for all subsequent political covenants, and his role as God's first earthly sovereign representative establishes the pattern of civil sovereignty over religious authority.

memes.label: Adam
memes.description: Adam appears in Leviathan as the original human being whose condition before and after the Fall illuminates Hobbes' arguments about the state of nature, paternal dominion, and the origin of sin.

memes.label: Apostles
memes.description: The Apostles in Leviathan were teachers and baptizers — not governors. Hobbes argues that the Apostolic office was temporary and conferred no governing authority, undermining claims of apostolic succession as a basis for ecclesiastical jurisdiction.

memes.label: Aristotle
memes.description: Aristotle (384–322 BC), referred to by Hobbes simply as "the Philosopher," is the principal philosophical adversary of Leviathan — Hobbes systematically rejects Aristotle's metaphysics, ethics, and politics while building his own system on anti-Aristotelian foundations.

memes.label: Ascension
memes.description: The Ascension of Christ is discussed in Leviathan as the event marking the transition from Christ's earthly ministry to the apostolic age — Hobbes argues that after the Ascension, Christ exercises no kingly office on earth until his Second Coming.

memes.label: Assembly
memes.description: An Assembly in Leviathan is a group of men authorized to deliberate and decide — Hobbes distinguishes between sovereign assemblies (which rule) and subordinate assemblies (which advise or administer), with all assemblies ultimately deriving their authority from the civil sovereign.

memes.label: Babylon
memes.description: Babylon appears in Leviathan in two forms — as the historical empire that destroyed Jerusalem and as the apocalyptic symbol of the Papacy in the Book of Revelation. Hobbes uses both to reinforce his argument against ecclesiastical independence.

memes.label: Baptism
memes.description: Baptism is discussed in Leviathan as a sacrament of initiation into the Kingdom of God — Hobbes argues that baptism does not confer spiritual authority independent of civil sovereignty.

memes.label: Canaan
memes.description: Canaan — the Promised Land — is the territorial basis of God's kingdom by covenant. Hobbes treats the conquest and settlement of Canaan as the establishment of a civil commonwealth under God's sovereignty.

memes.label: Christian Commonwealth
memes.description: The Christian Commonwealth is Hobbes' model of the proper relationship between church and state — a commonwealth in which the civil sovereign is also the supreme ecclesiastical authority, eliminating conflict between temporal and spiritual power.

memes.label: Church
memes.description: In Leviathan, a church is "a company of men professing Christian Religion, united in the person of one Soveraign" — Hobbes' definition makes the church entirely dependent on the civil sovereign for its unity and its legal existence.

memes.label: Church of Rome
memes.description: The Church of Rome is the principal institutional antagonist of Parts III and IV of Leviathan — Hobbes argues that the papacy's claim to universal jurisdiction is a usurpation of civil sovereignty and the "Kingdome of Darknesse."

memes.label: Cicero
memes.description: Cicero is referenced in Leviathan primarily through Hobbes' discussion of personhood — the Latin term "Persona" and Cicero's famous phrase "Unus sustineo tres Personas" provide the etymological foundation for Hobbes' theory of artificial persons and representation.

memes.label: Circumcision
memes.description: Circumcision is Hobbes' paradigm for the Old Covenant Sacrament of Admission — a visible sign of allegiance to God's sovereignty over Abraham and his seed, later replaced by baptism under the New Covenant.

memes.label: Common-wealth
memes.description: In Hobbes' Leviathan, the Common-wealth is the artificial person created when a multitude of men covenant together to confer all their power upon one representative, generating the "Mortal God" under which peace and common defence become possible.

memes.label: Consecration
memes.description: Consecration in Leviathan is the act of setting apart persons or things for divine service — Hobbes argues that the power to consecrate belongs ultimately to the civil sovereign as supreme pastor of the commonwealth.

memes.label: Conventicle
memes.description: A conventicle in Leviathan is an unauthorized religious assembly — Hobbes argues that any religious meeting not authorized by the civil sovereign is unlawful, because it represents a competing centre of authority within the commonwealth.

memes.label: Covenant
memes.description: In Hobbes' Leviathan, a covenant is a mutual transfer of right where one or both parties trust the other to perform in the future, forming the basis of all political obligation and the generation of the commonwealth.

memes.label: Creation
memes.description: The Creation is discussed in Leviathan primarily as the foundation of God's natural kingdom — by creating the world and all mankind, God establishes his irresistible power and thereby his natural right to rule over all.

memes.label: David
memes.description: King David is cited repeatedly in Leviathan as the paradigmatic sovereign by God's covenant — Hobbes uses David's reign to demonstrate that even Israel's kings held undivided authority over both civil and religious matters.

memes.label: Dominion
memes.description: In Hobbes' Leviathan, dominion is sovereign power acquired by force — either through conquest (despotical dominion) or through generation (paternal dominion) — whose rights and consequences are identical to sovereignty by institution.

memes.label: Egypt
memes.description: Egypt appears throughout Leviathan as the paradigmatic foreign kingdom — the place of Israel's bondage from which God delivered them, and the model of a commonwealth not under God's covenant.

memes.label: Episcopacy
memes.description: Episcopacy — church government by bishops — is treated in Leviathan as a legitimate form of church governance when authorized by the civil sovereign, but not as a divinely mandated form of church government.

memes.label: Excommunication
memes.description: Excommunication in Leviathan is the exclusion of a person from the company of the faithful — Hobbes argues that this is a spiritual penalty without temporal consequences, and that only the civil sovereign can impose civil penalties.

memes.label: Fear
memes.description: In Hobbes' Leviathan, fear is the passion that most powerfully motivates human behaviour — it is the origin of society, the basis of covenant-keeping, and the foundation of political obligation.

memes.label: Gospel
memes.description: The Gospel in Leviathan is the announcement of Christ's kingdom — Hobbes argues that the Gospel is a proclamation of a future kingdom, not a present government, and therefore confers no temporal authority on its ministers.

memes.label: Heaven
memes.description: Heaven in Leviathan is discussed in materialist and political terms — Hobbes distinguishes between the physical heavens (the sky), the Kingdom of Heaven (God's civil kingdom on earth), and the heaven of the blessed (life in the resurrected body on a renewed earth).

memes.label: Hell
memes.description: Hell in Leviathan is interpreted in materialist and political terms — Hobbes argues that the biblical "Hell" refers to the grave or to a future state of punishment after the resurrection, not to an eternal spiritual torment of disembodied souls.

memes.label: Honour
memes.description: In Hobbes' Leviathan, honour is the acknowledgment of power — a social phenomenon through which individuals express their estimation of another's power, forming the basis of social hierarchy and civil order.

memes.label: Independency
memes.description: Independency (Congregationalism) is discussed in Leviathan as the claim that each congregation is self-governing — Hobbes criticizes it as tending to anarchy because it allows every private judgment to challenge civil authority.

memes.label: Israel
memes.description: Israel in *Leviathan* is Hobbes' model of a kingdom of God by covenant — the historical instance of a commonwealth in which God Himself was the civil sovereign, administered through prophets and priests, providing the template for all subsequent Christian political theology.

memes.label: Jerusalem
memes.description: Jerusalem is the most significant place in Leviathan's biblical politics — as the seat of David's throne and the location of the Temple, it represents the earthly centre of God's kingdom, which Hobbes interprets as a civil commonwealth.

memes.label: Jesus Christ
memes.description: Jesus Christ is the central theological figure of Parts III and IV of Leviathan — Hobbes argues that Christ's kingdom is "not of this world," meaning that ecclesiastical authority is purely spiritual and must be subordinated to civil sovereignty in all temporal matters.

memes.label: Job
memes.description: Job is the most important biblical figure for Hobbes' theodicy — the Book of Job demonstrates God's absolute and unaccountable sovereignty, which Hobbes takes as the model for all civil sovereignty.

memes.label: River Jordan
memes.description: The River Jordan appears in Leviathan as the boundary of the Promised Land and the site of John's baptism — a geographical marker in Hobbes' biblical politics.

memes.label: Joseph
memes.description: Joseph, the son of Jacob, appears in Leviathan as an example of how God's sovereignty operates through human agents — his rule over Egypt under Pharaoh demonstrates that God's representatives exercise civil, not merely spiritual, authority.

memes.label: Joshua
memes.description: Joshua, Moses' successor, appears in Leviathan as an example of sovereign leadership by divine commission — his conquest of Canaan demonstrates that even total war is justified when commanded by the sovereign representative of God.

memes.label: Judah
memes.description: The Kingdom of Judah — the southern kingdom after the division of Israel — appears in *Leviathan* primarily in Hobbes' discussions of biblical history, prophecy, and the Davidic line from which the Messiah was to come.

memes.label: Last Judgement
memes.description: The Last Judgement is discussed in Leviathan as the event at which Christ will exercise his kingly office — Hobbes argues that Christ will reign as king on earth only after the resurrection and final judgement, not before.

memes.label: Justice
memes.description: In Hobbes' Leviathan, justice is defined strictly as the keeping of valid covenants; there is no justice prior to covenant, and no enforcement of justice without a sovereign power — making justice an artificial virtue dependent on the commonwealth.

memes.label: Kingdom of God
memes.description: The Kingdom of God is the central theological-political concept of Parts III and IV of Leviathan — Hobbes argues that God's kingdom is a civil kingdom, exercised through sovereign representatives on earth and culminating in Christ's reign after the resurrection.

memes.label: Knowledge
memes.description: Hobbes divides all knowledge into Knowledge of Fact (absolute, from sense and memory) and Science (conditional, from reasoning about causes and consequences) — a taxonomy that structures his entire philosophical system in Leviathan.

memes.label: Land of Promise
memes.description: The Land of Promise — the land promised by God to Abraham and his descendants — is the territorial foundation of God's covenant kingdom. Hobbes treats God's promise of land as the model for all sovereign grants of territory and jurisdiction.

memes.label: Law of Nature
memes.description: In Hobbes' Leviathan, a Law of Nature is a precept or general rule discovered by reason that forbids a man to do what is destructive of his life, forming the rational basis for peace-seeking behaviour that culminates in the social covenant.

memes.label: Levites
memes.description: The Levites were the tribe set apart for service in the Temple — Hobbes uses their subordination to the civil sovereign (Moses, then the Kings of Judah) as biblical proof that all ecclesiastical officers are subject to civil authority.

memes.label: Liberty
memes.description: In Hobbes' Leviathan, liberty is the absence of external impediments to motion — applied to human beings as the absence of legal and physical restraints, with the crucial consequence that fear and necessity are compatible with liberty.

memes.label: Marriage
memes.description: Marriage in Leviathan is treated as a civil contract whose terms and validity are determined by the civil law — Hobbes rejects the Catholic claim that marriage is a sacrament governed by canon law.

memes.label: Monarchy
memes.description: Monarchy — the rule of one — is Hobbes' preferred form of government, compared systematically with aristocracy and democracy in Chapter XIX of *Leviathan*, where Hobbes argues that monarchy minimises the inconveniences inherent in all commonwealths by institution.

memes.label: Moses
memes.description: Moses is the most frequently cited biblical figure in Leviathan — Hobbes treats him as the paradigmatic sovereign by institution, whose authority derived from the consent of the Israelites and whose role as God's representative prefigures civil sovereignty.

memes.label: Mount Sinai
memes.description: Mount Sinai is the place where God gave the Law to Moses and established his kingdom by covenant with Israel — for Hobbes, this is the founding moment of the Kingdom of God as a civil commonwealth.

memes.label: New Testament
memes.description: The New Testament — the Christian scriptures — is Hobbes' source for the teaching and institution of Christ's kingdom, which he reads as confirming that Christ's kingdom is not of this world and that Christians owe obedience to their civil sovereigns.

memes.label: Obligation
memes.description: In Hobbes' Leviathan, obligation arises from the voluntary transfer of right through covenant — it is the self-imposed bond that ties subjects to the sovereign and makes justice possible.

memes.label: Old Testament
memes.description: The Old Testament — the Hebrew scriptures — is Hobbes' principal source for the history of God's kingdom by covenant, providing the constitutional model for Israel as a commonwealth under divine sovereignty that is later renewed and transformed in the New Testament.

memes.label: Ordination
memes.description: Ordination in Leviathan is the appointment of ministers of the Gospel — Hobbes argues that ordination is an act of teaching authority only, and that the appointment of pastors in a Christian commonwealth is ultimately subject to the civil sovereign.

memes.label: Papacy
memes.description: The Papacy is treated in Leviathan as the "Kingdome of Darknesse" — a usurpation of civil sovereignty achieved through doctrinal corruption, institutional manipulation, and the systematic misinterpretation of scripture.

memes.label: Paradise
memes.description: Paradise in Leviathan is discussed in the context of Adam's original condition and of the future kingdom — Hobbes interprets the biblical Paradise in materialist terms, consistent with his rejection of disembodied spiritual existence.

memes.label: Pentecost
memes.description: Pentecost is discussed in Leviathan as the event at which the Holy Spirit descended upon the Apostles — Hobbes interprets this as the beginning of the apostolic teaching ministry, not as the founding of a separate ecclesiastical government.
aliases: The Descent of the Holy Ghost

memes.label: Peter (Apostle)
memes.description: The Apostle Peter is discussed in Leviathan primarily in connection with Christ's words "Feed my sheep" — Hobbes argues that this commission conferred teaching authority, not ruling authority, and therefore does not support papal claims to temporal power.

memes.label: Plato
memes.description: Plato is referenced in Leviathan as the source of the doctrine of separated essences which Hobbes rejects — but also as the origin of the phrase that "Law is the Publique Conscience," which Hobbes partially endorses.

memes.label: Power
memes.description: In Hobbes' Leviathan, power is a person's present means to obtain some future apparent good — divided into natural power (faculties of body and mind) and instrumental power (acquired means), with the greatest human power being the sovereign power of the commonwealth.

memes.label: Presbytery
memes.description: Presbyterian church government is discussed in Leviathan as an alternative to both episcopacy and independency — Hobbes criticizes it for dividing sovereignty by claiming an independent spiritual jurisdiction alongside the civil sovereign.

memes.label: Levitical Priesthood
memes.description: The Levitical Priesthood of the Old Testament is discussed extensively in Leviathan as the model of an ecclesiastical office subordinate to civil sovereignty — the High Priest exercised authority only as God's and the sovereign's lieutenant.

memes.label: Punishment
memes.description: In Hobbes' Leviathan, punishment is an evil inflicted by public authority for a transgression of the law, serving not as revenge but as a means to enforce obedience and preserve the commonwealth.

memes.label: Purgatory
memes.description: Purgatory in Leviathan is treated as a doctrinal invention of the Papacy — Hobbes argues that the doctrine has no biblical warrant and was created to generate revenue and extend ecclesiastical control over the laity.

memes.label: Reason
memes.description: In Hobbes' Leviathan, reason is not an innate faculty but the process of computation — addition and subtraction of consequences — through which humans discover the laws of nature and construct the artificial order of the commonwealth.

memes.label: Redemption
memes.description: Redemption in Leviathan is Christ's payment of the ransom for sin — Hobbes interprets redemption in legal and political terms as the satisfaction of a debt, consistent with his contractual theory of obligation.

memes.label: Representation
memes.description: In Hobbes' Leviathan, representation is the mechanism by which one person bears the person of another — the foundation of artificial personhood and the means through which the multitude becomes a unified commonwealth.

memes.label: Resurrection
memes.description: The Resurrection of the dead is a central Christian doctrine discussed extensively in Parts III and IV of Leviathan — Hobbes argues that the resurrection will be of bodies, not souls, and that the Kingdom of God after the resurrection will be a civil kingdom on earth.

memes.label: Roman Empire
memes.description: The Roman Empire appears in Leviathan as a historical exemplar of absolute sovereignty and as the political infrastructure that the Papacy appropriated — Hobbes famously describes the Papacy as "the Ghost of the deceased Romane Empire."

memes.label: Rome
memes.description: Rome appears in Leviathan in two guises — as the capital of the Roman Empire (the model of civil sovereignty) and as the seat of the Papacy (the counterfeit of that sovereignty). Hobbes' analysis of the Papacy is inseparable from his analysis of Rome.

memes.label: Sabbath
memes.description: The Sabbath is discussed in Leviathan as a ceremonial law whose observance was commanded in the Old Testament but whose authority in Christian commonwealths is determined by the civil sovereign.

memes.label: Sacrament
memes.description: Sacraments in Leviathan are signs of admission into Christ's kingdom — Hobbes argues that their administration is a teaching function, not a governing function, and therefore falls under the ultimate authority of the civil sovereign.

memes.label: Sacrifice
memes.description: Sacrifice in *Leviathan* spans Old Testament ritual offerings and Christ's self-sacrifice on the cross — Hobbes treats both as acts of obedience within a covenantal framework, subordinating priestly sacrifice to civil sovereignty.

memes.label: Samuel
memes.description: The prophet Samuel is crucial to Hobbes' argument about the dangers of divided sovereignty — Samuel's warning to the Israelites about the ways of kings is analyzed by Hobbes as a description of absolute sovereign power, not a condemnation of it.

memes.label: Saul
memes.description: Saul, the first king of Israel, appears in Leviathan as an example of God's sovereign right to appoint and depose rulers — his rejection by God for disobedience illustrates the limits of human authority over divine command but not the limits of sovereign power over subjects.

memes.label: School (Philosophical)
memes.description: The philosophical schools — particularly the Scholastics and Aristotelians of the universities — are Hobbes' principal targets in his critique of vain philosophy, which he identifies as a primary source of spiritual darkness and civil disorder in Parts I and IV of *Leviathan*.

memes.label: Scripture (Holy Bible)
memes.description: The Holy Scripture — comprising the Old and New Testaments — is Hobbes' supreme textual authority in *Leviathan*, serving simultaneously as a source of divine law, a historical record of God's kingdom, and the foundation upon which he builds his argument for the subordination of ecclesiastical to civil power.

memes.label: Sin
memes.description: The transgression of a law — in Hobbes' Leviathan, sin is defined in relation to law, distinguished from crime by the forum of judgment, and resolved only through obedience to the sovereign and faith in Christ.

memes.label: Sodom
memes.description: Sodom appears in Leviathan as the paradigmatic city destroyed by divine judgement — Hobbes uses the story of Sodom to illustrate the relationship between divine sovereignty and human wickedness.

memes.label: Solomon
memes.description: King Solomon appears in Leviathan as an example of sovereign wisdom and sovereign failure — Hobbes cites Solomon's Proverbs as authority for his political arguments while also using Solomon's idolatry to demonstrate that even the wisest sovereign can err in religious matters.

memes.label: Sovereign
memes.description: In Hobbes' Leviathan, the Sovereign is the artificial person who bears the person of the commonwealth, holding absolute and indivisible sovereign power conferred by the covenant of every man with every man.

memes.label: St. John
memes.description: St. John the Evangelist is invoked by Hobbes primarily for his teachings on the Incarnation, the testing of spirits, and the nature of Christ — serving as a key authority in Hobbes' argument against the doctrine of the Trinity and for the mortalist interpretation of the afterlife.

memes.label: St. Paul
memes.description: The Apostle Paul is Hobbes' most frequently cited New Testament authority in *Leviathan* — invoked on matters of obedience, church governance, resurrection, and the nature of Christ's kingdom, always to support Hobbes' argument for undivided civil sovereignty.

memes.label: Synagogue
memes.description: The Synagogue appears in Leviathan as the model of a religious assembly under civil authority — Hobbes argues that synagogues were always subject to the sovereign (whether Moses, the High Priest, or the King), establishing the pattern for Christian churches.

memes.label: Temple
memes.description: The Temple in Jerusalem is discussed extensively in Leviathan as the centre of worship in God's kingdom — Hobbes argues that the Temple was built by the civil sovereign (Solomon) and that the priests served in it under the sovereign's authority.

memes.label: Universities
memes.description: Universities appear in Leviathan primarily as objects of criticism — Hobbes argues that they have been "the Core of Rebellion" by teaching Aristotelian philosophy and doctrines that undermine civil sovereignty.

memes.label: Warre
memes.description: In Hobbes' Leviathan, war is not merely battle but the known disposition to fight — a condition in which no security is possible and which constitutes the natural condition of mankind outside civil society.

memes.label: Wilderness
memes.description: The Wilderness of the Exodus — where Israel wandered for forty years — appears in Leviathan as the transitional space between Egypt (bondage) and Canaan (the promised kingdom), and as the setting for the giving of the Law at Sinai.
